era_context: The Middle Bronze Age II in the Levant and Egypt; the Middle Kingdom in Egypt under the 12th Dynasty (Amenemhat III's reign, c. 1860–1814 BCE). In Mesopotamia, the Old Babylonian period is in decline; Hammurabi's dynasty (c. 1792–1750 BCE) has not yet risen to prominence. The Indus Valley Civilization remains at its mature phase. Minoan Crete (Middle Minoan II–III) is flourishing. This is a period of regional consolidation and trade network expansion across the Eastern Mediterranean and Indian Ocean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for this period)
Northern Europe (pre-literate societies; archaeological record is thin)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating is imprecise)

events.title: Reign of Amenemhat III continues; Nile flood records and administrative activity
events.description: Amenemhat III, one of Egypt's most successful Middle Kingdom pharaohs, continues his reign. This period is marked by extensive administrative records, temple construction, and careful management of Nile inundation cycles. The Fayum region undergoes significant irrigation development and settlement expansion. Amenemhat III's reign is well-attested through administrative papyri, temple inscriptions, and the Lahun pyramid complex.

sources.name: Administrative papyri from Lahun and Kahun
Temple inscriptions and stelae from Bubastis and other sites
Nile flood records (Nilometer data and administrative records)

events.certainty_note: Amenemhat III's reign is securely dated to c. 1860–1814 BCE by Egyptian chronology. The year 1956 BCE falls within this period with high confidence.

events.title: Minoan Crete in Middle Minoan II–III period; palace complexes and trade networks
events.description: Minoan Crete during this period is characterized by the flourishing of palace complexes (Knossos, Phaistos, Malia) and extensive maritime trade networks. Pottery production and artistic styles show sophisticated craftsmanship. Trade connections extend to Egypt, the Levant, and Anatolia. This is a period of cultural efflorescence and economic prosperity for Minoan civilization.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from palace excavations at Knossos, Phaistos, and Malia
Minoan pottery typology and stratigraphic analysis

events.certainty_note: Minoan chronology is debated. The conventional Middle Minoan II–III dating places this period around 1900–1700 BCE, but some scholars propose alternative chronologies. Assignment to 1956 BCE is approximate within a ±50-year range.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Minoan Crete remains contested. Some scholars argue for a 'high chronology' that would place Middle Minoan II–III earlier; others support a 'low chronology' that would place it later. The Thera eruption, traditionally linked to the end of Middle Minoan III, has been variously dated from c. 1650 to 1500 BCE, creating uncertainty about the precise chronological placement of events in this year. Egyptian chronology itself, while relatively secure for the 12th Dynasty, has minor variations in scholarly literature (±10–20 years).
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1956 BCE is heavily weighted toward Egypt and the Eastern Mediterranean, where written records and archaeological evidence are abundant. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Central Asia are virtually absent from the historical record for this year. The Indus Valley Civilization, though contemporary and sophisticated, left few datable records for specific years. Mesopotamian records for this period are sparse, as the Old Babylonian period is in transition. The surviving sources are biased toward monumental and administrative records; daily life and economic activity are underrepresented except where administrative papyri provide glimpses.

graph_edges.note: Amenemhat III's reign ends c. 1814 BCE, marking the end of the 12th Dynasty's peak power.
Minoan palace culture continues until the Late Minoan period, c. 1700 BCE and beyond.
